Reviews Analysed
3,942
Aer Lingus · British Airways · Iberia, 2014–2026
AL Rating: Peak vs Now
6.35 ↓ 2.79
2015 high vs 2022–2025 average
Wouldn’t Recommend AL
83%
Among 2022–2025 reviewers
01
“Aer Lingus lost 1.66 rating points in a single year”

Between 2017 and 2018, Aer Lingus suffered the steepest single-year rating collapse of any of the three airlines in the dataset — dropping from 5.29 to 3.62 in twelve months. Neither British Airways nor Iberia saw anything comparable: BA actually improved slightly (+0.22), and Iberia moved marginally (−0.20). AL has never recovered. From 2018 onward it has plateaued in a 2.4–3.6 band, reaching a low of 2.39 in 2022 post-pandemic.

02
“Historically the leader; recently bottom of the table”

Across the full 2014–2026 dataset, Aer Lingus holds the highest mean overall rating of the three airlines (5.24 vs BA 4.22 vs IB 4.52). But filter to 2022–2025 and the picture inverts completely: AL drops to 2.79, trailing both BA (3.60) and IB (3.01). AL’s recommendation rate has collapsed from 60% (2015–2017) to just 17% today. BA, despite never being loved, has remained broadly stable. Iberia has recovered from its own 2019–2020 nadir.

03
“VFM correlates with overall rating at r = 0.86–0.88 across all three airlines”

Of all six service dimensions tracked — seat comfort, cabin staff, food, ground service, IFE, and value for money — VFM is the single strongest predictor of overall rating. The implication is clear: passengers aren’t primarily reacting to seat width or food quality — they’re reacting to whether they feel the price was worth it. In the 2022–2025 period AL’s VFM has collapsed from 3.23 to 2.01.

AL Sub-metric Averages (All Years)
VFM: Peak vs Recent
04
“Economy passengers are 2.4× more likely to leave a negative review than Business”

Business Class passengers consistently rate higher across all three airlines, but the gap and what it implies differ. Aer Lingus Business scores 6.76 vs Economy’s 5.08 — the clearest premium signal, and at a 70% recommendation rate, AL Business passengers are broadly satisfied. Iberia shows the most extreme split: Business at 6.44 vs Economy at 3.75, a gap of +2.69. BA’s Business product at 4.82 is the weakest premium offering of the three, barely clearing “average” despite long-haul pricing.

05
“Couples are the most critical traveller segment — 71% of AL couple reviews are negative”

Solo leisure travellers are the most forgiving reviewers across all three airlines, consistently rating highest (AL 4.75, BA 4.68, IB 4.20). Couples are markedly harsher — AL drops to 3.43 among couples (71% negative), and Iberia couple leisure sits at 2.73, effectively a condemnation of the product. Business travellers rate BA worst of all segments at just 3.31, pointing to a specific failure to meet corporate expectations. Families sit in the middle but trend negative for AL (68% negative, 3.71 mean).

06
“Ground Service fell furthest; VFM and Cabin Staff followed close behind”

Comparing Aer Lingus’s sub-metric scores between its 2015–2017 peak and the 2022–2025 recent period, every single dimension has deteriorated. The sharpest decline is Ground Service (−1.33 points), followed by VFM (−1.22) and Cabin Staff Service (−1.04). Even the most resilient metric — Inflight Entertainment — fell by −0.59. This cross-metric deterioration rules out a single-cause explanation: it’s not just a food quality problem, or just a pricing problem. The decline is systemic.

Analysis based on 3,942 verified and unverified reviews scraped from Skytrax covering Aer Lingus (n=1,044), British Airways (n=2,000), and Iberia (n=898) for flights operated between 2014 and 2026. Ratings are on a 1–10 scale for Overall and 1–5 for sub-metrics. Year attribution is by Date Flown, not review date.
